issue_body:
The GExiv2 metadata library can read data from a file or from image data already in memory. The latter method is very slow when using pgi. The following test program reports an elapsed time of about 3 to 4 milliseconds without pgi, 3 to 4 seconds with! I get similar results with Python 2 or Python 3. (The image file is a full resolution JPEG straight from my EOS 100D DSLR.)

Simple test program:
```
from __future__ import print_function

import time

import pgi
pgi.install_as_gi()
from gi.repository import GObject, GExiv2

with open('/home/jim/Pictures/from_camera/2015/2015_10_20/IMGc0983.JPG', 'rb') as pf:
    image_data = pf.read()
md = GExiv2.Metadata()
start = time.time()
md.open_buf(image_data)
stop = time.time()
print('Elapsed time', stop - start)
```

issue_title: HTTPError not caught as expected in flower.views.auth.LoginHandler
issue_body:
This is a small but annoying bug that occurs in the `_on_auth()` method on `flower.views.auth.LoginHandler` (related to PR #359).  When `tornado.web.HTTPError` is raised, the expected behaviour is that the error is caught and handled by displaying the error message to the user using a template file. Instead, the exception is logged to stderr, and nothing is displayed to the user, causing the request to hang.

I suspect this is because `_on_auth()` is executed as a callback in `get_authenticated_user()` (which is itself defined as an instance method on `tornado.auth.GoogleOAuth2Mixin`), which means it's being converted into a future by the `tornado.auth._auth_return_future` decorator in a way that causes exceptions to be handled differently. I'd love to hear any thoughts on what's happening under the hood and if there's a way to force HTTPError to be handled as expected, as I can't find a good fix for this.

issue_title: Flower leaks tracebacks on HTTP 500 in production mode
issue_body: Currently flower leaks full tracebacks to any user on HTTP 500 when `debug` is `False`. It used to be worse; until #633 got merged, flower was leaking configuration as well. issue_title: Bundle not included in RDF seralisation
issue_body:
Hi @trungdong, @satra,

With the RDF exporter, it looks like bundles are exported as if they were entities. Here is a minimal example:

```
import prov.model as prov

document = prov.ProvDocument()

document.set_default_namespace('http://example.org/0/')
document.add_namespace('ex1', 'http://example.org/1/')
document.add_namespace('ex2', 'http://example.org/2/')

document.entity('e001')

bundle = document.bundle('e001')
bundle.set_default_namespace('http://example.org/2/')
bundle.entity('e001')

print(document.get_provn())

print('---')

print(document.serialize(format='rdf', rdf_format='turtle'))
```

And the output is:
```
document
  default <http://example.org/0/>
  prefix ex2 <http://example.org/2/>
  prefix ex1 <http://example.org/1/>
  
  entity(e001)
  bundle e001
    default <http://example.org/2/>
    
    entity(e001)
  endBundle
endDocument
---
@prefix ex1: <http://example.org/1/> .
@prefix ex2: <http://example.org/2/> .
@prefix prov: <http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

<http://example.org/0/e001> a prov:Entity .

ex2:e001 a prov:Entity .
```

There is [no formal representation of bundles in turtle (we should use trig instead)](https://github.com/lucmoreau/ProvToolbox/issues/107) but I was nevertheless expecting `ex001` entity to be declared as a `prov:Bundle` in the RDF-turtle serialisation, i.e.:
```
@prefix ex1: <http://example.org/1/> .
@prefix ex2: <http://example.org/2/> .
@prefix prov: <http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

ex:e001 a prov:Bundle .

ex2:e001 a prov:Entity .
```

What are you thought on this? Thanks in advance for your feedback!

Hi,

I often need to read single header keywords from thousands of FITS files.

What I did in the beginning was using pyfits and read every single header and fetch the information that I need. So something like this:
```
from astropy.io import fits
hdr = [fits.getheader(f) for f in files]
```
I just did a test, and with 7000 FITS files this takes about 70 seconds.

So what I'm doing at the moment is a system call to dfits/fitsort, i.e. something like this:
```
dfits = subprocess.Popen(['dfits'] + files, stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
fitsort = subprocess.check_output(['fitsort'] + keywords, stdin=dfits.stdout).decode('utf-8')
dfits.wait()
```
This way, it only takes about 7 seconds to get the data from all files. But, as a downside, I need to now the keywords that I need before running this command.

Now I' m trying the same with fitsio, hoping for some improvement in speed as compared to pyfits. Getting close to the dfits/fitsort version would be nice. So this is what I do:
```
import fitsio
hdr = [fitsio.read_header(s) for s in args.spectra]
```
Unfortunately this also takes about 50 seconds, so not nearly as fast as I was hoping for.

Is there any other way to get this being done faster? Thanks.

Cheers,
Tim

issue_title: Circular reference between Connection and Cursor
issue_body: Each cursor stores reference to it's connection. And each connection stores reference to active (Connection._active_cursor) cursor. Both classes define __del__ method. On Python 3 I've got the following error while trying to send any mail: `503 Error: send HELO first`.

I managed to investigate the source and it appears to be as follows.

You subclass `smtpd.SMTPChannel` and override the method `smtp_EHLO`.
In the CPython implementation of this method the instance variable `self.seen_greeting` is set and then, in the `smtp_RCPT` method, there is a check on that same variable ([relevant code here](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.4/Lib/smtpd.py#l501)).
This check fails and trigger the error.

As far as I can see, there is no need to override the `smtp_EHLO` method.

issue_title: Inotify.__handle_inotify_event raises another TypeError when attempting to handle an event in python3.
issue_body:
Having fixed the two problems recently reported, another TypeError is raised while handling an event.

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/inotify/dev/test.py", line 50, in <module>
    _main()
  File "/inotify/dev/test.py", line 38, in _main
    for event in i.event_gen():
  File "/inotify/inotify/adapters.py", line 162, in event_gen
    in self.__handle_inotify_event(fd, event_type):
  File "/inotify/inotify/adapters.py", line 141, in __handle_inotify_event
    filename = filename.rstrip('\0')
TypeError: 'str' does not support the buffer interface

Here, the problem is that filename is a 'bytes' object in python 3, so needs to be decoded before stripping.  My fix was this:

```
filename = filename.encode(sys.getdefaultencoding()).rstrip('\0')
```

ELF error: Unsupported relocation type: 2
```

The reason for that is because there are no relocation recipes for ARM in elftools/elf/relocation.py only ones exist for MIPS and X64 and X86.

The complete list is here:
http://infocenter.arm.com/help/topic/com.arm.doc.ihi0044e/IHI0044E_aaelf.pdf table 4-8.

As a quick hack we can lie and say that this is a MIPS file and this works ok (at least on this file) but it would be good to have proper support.

```
class ELFFile(elffile.ELFFile):
    def get_machine_arch(self):
        result = super(ELFFile, self).get_machine_arch()
        if result == "ARM":
            result = "MIPS"

        return result

elffile.ELFFile = ELFFile
```

When send_message is called with wait_for_response set to True, the callback is registered after the message is sent.
This means there's a risk of wait_for_response never returning if the message reply has already been received before the callback is registered.

A proposed patch is included below.

Patch:
```
diff --git a/pychromecast/socket_client.py b/pychromecast/socket_client.py
index ee76f9e..5655f3f 100644
--- a/pychromecast/socket_client.py
+++ b/pychromecast/socket_client.py
@@ -550,8 +550,14 @@ class SocketClient(threading.Thread):
             raise PyChromecastStopped("Socket client's thread is stopped.")
         if not self.connecting and not self._force_recon:
             try:
+                callback = self._request_callbacks[request_id] = {
+                    'event': threading.Event(),
+                    'response': None,
+                }
                 self.socket.sendall(be_size + msg.SerializeToString())
             except socket.error:
+                del self._request_callbacks[request_id]
+                wait_for_response = False
                 self._force_recon = True
                 self.logger.info('Error writing to socket.')
         else:
@@ -559,10 +565,6 @@ class SocketClient(threading.Thread):

         response = None
         if not no_add_request_id and wait_for_response:
-            callback = self._request_callbacks[request_id] = {
-                'event': threading.Event(),
-                'response': None,
-            }
             callback['event'].wait()
             response = callback.get('response')
         return response
```

issue_title: Restrict paths accepted by ConfigManager.get and ConfigManager.set
issue_body:
Valid paths:
 * `('section', 'option')`
 * `('section', 'subsection', 'option')`
 * `('prefix.root.suffix', 'option')`

Tricky paths (valid, but not interpreted as originally):
 * `('section.option')` -- should be interpreted as `c.section == 'DEFAULT'` and `c.option == 'section.option'`.

This is in order to stay closer to standard library's `ConfigParser` and to allow things like domain names in section and option names which is the case in some `ConfigParser` examples in official documentation: https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/configparser.html#quick-start

If is_default is meant to answer the question of whether the effective value is the default value then it should return True only if the configured environment variable is not set.

Perhaps that's why providing such nice helpers is not always a great idea -- the more cross-features you add, the harder it becomes to support them.

issue_title: Missing argument in write_blank for format.set_center_across() example
issue_body:
Missing argument for ```worksheet.write_blank``` in the [documentation](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/format.html#set_center_across) for ```format.set_center_across()```.

The example uses: 
```py
worksheet.write_blank(1, 2, format)
```
```write_blank``` requires a None or empty string argument though (which is ignored). So the above line should instead read:
```py
worksheet.write_blank(1, 2, None, format)
```

In #103 I described a problem with the current handling of Transit hints, such that two clients who offer different transit servers will fail to connect, and a client who offers two or more transit servers which actually map to the same host will cause the other client to connect to itself (and fail to connect to their partner)

To fix this, we need:

* define a new relay handshake that includes a side identifier
* upgrade the relay server to accept either form of handshake
* redefine the old handshake as providing an "unsided" side identifier
* change the relay server to connect two clients who use the same handshake key, but different side identifiers. Unsided connections can be connected to anything, including another unsided connection.
* change the client to send sided handshakes
* change the client to de-duplicate relay servers (by storing them in a set)
* change client to include its own (offered) relay server in the set that it connects to

issue_title: read dataframe from and write dataframe to a pickle file
issue_body: It would be convenient to be able to load and save pandas dataframes that are stored in the form of a pickle file (e.g. the result of something like `pickle.dump(df, open('outfile', 'wb'))`).
As discussed in #52, is there any way that dataclasses and attrs could share a protocol that would make things like serialization easily shareable between them?

It would be helpful to have a list of functional differences between `dataclasses` and `attrs`, broken down by `@dataclass` vs `@attr.s` and `field` vs `attr.ib`. 

This would be useful and illuminating for a few reasons:

---

It would make it easier to vet the logic behind, and need for, each of the proposed differences.  

@hynek and @Tinche have invested years of thought into the current design:  deviating from it without fully understanding the history and reasoning behind each decision might lead to this project needlessly repeating mistakes.   I'm glad to see that the `attrs` devs have already been brought into several issues.  My hope is we can get a bird's eye view so that nothing slips through the cracks.

---

If the differences aren't too great (and ideally they will not be, see above) I'd like to see a `dataclass` compatibility mode for `attrs` (e.g. `from attrs import dataclass, field`).  

I'm glad that this badly-needed feature is being worked on, but sadly I'm stuck in python 2 for at least another 2 years, so it's important to me, and surely many `attrs`-users, to have an easy path to adoption once this becomes part of stdlib.

I forked and added a small patch, https://github.com/aptwebapps/django-jsonview/commit/447f240bef1cb288a24888f70a90f8e04e0bc945 , to enable custom encoders. I'd send a pull request, but it needs polishing (tests, Readme updates).  Before I do that, are you interested in the idea?

issue_title: Propagate API exceptions in Django backend

With batch sending all API exceptions are hidden, because API responds with a list of results and 200 HTTP code. Only non 4xx and 5xx codes will cause an exception.

In case if there is one or more errors in batch response, then exception should be risen (with appropriate ``fail_silently`` logic).

Probably, multiple errors could be combined in a single one.

issue_title: poor conversion between lchab and srgb
issue_body:
lchab may be converted to value that is not optimal. For example LCHabColor(89, 7, 75) is converted to rgb(231, 222, 211) but for example rgb(233,222,211) would be a better target.

rgb(231, 222, 211) converted back to lchab is (88.9, 6.8, 79.8)
rgb(233, 222, 211) converted back to lchab is (88.7, 6.9, 74.2) what is closer to (89, 7, 75)

It is also possible that rgb to lchab conversion is broken. According to http://www.brucelindbloom.com/index.html?Math.html lchab -> srgb conversion works and srgb -> lchab is broken.

Curiously, http://www.colorhexa.com/ has different results for lchab -> srgb conversion but the same for srgb -> lchab.

According to brucelindbloom lchab(89, 7, 75) = rgb(231.3, 222.1, 210.7)
According to colormath it is (231, 222, 211)
According to colorhexa.com it is (233, 222, 211).

According to brucelindbloom rgb(231, 222, 211) = lchab(89.0, 6.8, 74.7)
According to colormath it is lchab(88.6, 6.7, 79.5)
According to colorhexa.com it is lchab(88.9, 6.6, 79.5)

Either there is something broken somewhere or I am missing parameter that is set differently in these tools. I see no way to set reference white and adaptation in python-colormath and colorhexa, so it seems that d50 and Bradford may be assumed to be defaults. sRGB nearly certainly may be assumed to be default in colorhexa.

```
from colormath.color_conversions import convert_color
from colormath.color_objects import LabColor, LCHabColor, SpectralColor, sRGBColor, \
XYZColor, LCHuvColor, IPTColor


lch = LCHabColor(89, 7, 75)
rgb = convert_color(lch, sRGBColor)
print(rgb.get_upscaled_value_tuple())
lch = convert_color(rgb, LCHabColor)
print(lch.get_value_tuple())

other_rgb = sRGBColor(233.0/256,222.0/256,211.0/256)
lch = convert_color(other_rgb, LCHabColor)
print(lch.get_value_tuple())

other_rgb = sRGBColor(231.0/256,222.0/256,211.0/256)
lch = convert_color(other_rgb, LCHabColor)
print(lch.get_value_tuple())
```

issue_title: Difference between knn and normal face recognition
issue_body:
The dlib dnn face recognition sample compares a found face in a picture with pre-found faces and decides that two faces are identical if the difference between the two is less than a certain threshold.

As far as I can understand, you do the same with the following command:

`$ face_recognition ./pictures_of_people_i_know/ ./unknown_pictures/`

That means, if I want to match a face to a database of faces, I have to compare the face with all the faces in the database until I find one with a diffence less than the desired threshold value.

Is the knn sample trying to make this process faster? If yes, how is it doing this?

@mock.patch('path.to.my.mocked.method')

issue_title: Please package the next version as v1.20
issue_body:
Hi, I'm working on updating the packaging for python-json-patch in Debian, and it looks like at some point in the past, we had packaged v1.10 as v1.19 (I'm not sure if this was a previous Debian maintainer making a typo, or a v1.19 tag in the upstream repo - we have [a git commit that claims to merge the v1.19 tag](https://anonscm.debian.org/cgit/openstack/python/python-json-patch.git/commit/?h=debian/liberty&id=586cda77ff999a096347e61c2f1807dc61221d2b), but no such tag currently exists in your repo). This mistake has also propagated to downstream distributions including Ubuntu.

Since we need version numbers to increase for upgrades to happen, Ubuntu is working around this by calling the latest version "1.19+really1.16", which sorts after "1.19" (see https://pad.lv/1704024), and I'll do the same in Debian.

Do you mind giving the next release the version number 1.20, so that we can resynchronize and get onto reasonable version numbers? Sorry about the trouble, and thanks for your software!
It's over three years old. It was released 2013-02-26

inline-requests version 0.1.2 you could decorate callback with inline_requests decorator and it worked ok. In 0.3.1 version  this no longer works

```python
from inline_requests import inline_requests

    @inline_requests

```

traceback is:

# What
I’d like to propose that we use PR template in MXNet. The template should consist of a checklist for the required items for merging a PR (e.g. lint and testing), as well as the items of the change (e.g. features)

# Why
It should serve two purposes:
- It reminds both the contributor and reviewer/committers of checking the prerequisites before merging.
- It serves as a progress tracking tool on which PRs are ready for review and merging, so that reviewer/committers 

# How
- Contributor should maintain the status of PR by checking/unchecking items in the checklist.
- Committer should uncheck items that hasn’t been passed yet (e.g. contributor checked lint but lint failed on CI), and make comments on what more should be done. By default committers review PRs that are shown to be complete in the PR list.

# FAQ
- What if I as a contributor want to request for review of things working in progress?
Everyone can still do what everyone is doing now: requesting reviewer to take a look by @ them.
- What if some items in the checklist don’t apply to my PR?
They can be deleted.

# Template
## Description ##
(Brief description on what this PR is about)

## Checklist ##
### Essentials ###
- [ ] Passed code style checking (`make lint`)
- [ ] Changes are complete (i.e. I finished coding on this PR)
- [ ] All changes have test coverage
- [ ] Examples are either not affected by this change, or have been fixed to be compatible with this change
- [ ] For user-facing API changes, API doc string has been updated.

### Changes ###
- [ ] Feature1, tests, (and when applicable, API doc)
- [ ] Feature2, tests, (and when applicable, API doc)

## Comments ##

- Intersting edge cases to note here

issue_title: New padding value in max Pooling
issue_body:
The padding behaviour of max Pooling layer has changed at some point, padding with a value of -3.4e+38 (probably the most negative float) instead of zero.

While the reason for this is understandable (i.e. never pick the padding value as output), it leads to very undesirable side effects for the cases in which the kernel size is less or equal the padding size. Namely, the huge negative padding value makes it to the output.

```
inp = mx.symbol.Variable('input')
sym = mx.symbol.Pooling(data=inp, kernel=(2,2), pool_type='max', pad=(2,2))

data = mx.ndarray.random_uniform(shape=(1,1,2,2))

executor = sym.bind(mx.cpu(), {'input': data})
out = executor.forward()
print data.asnumpy()
print( out[0].asnumpy() )
```

output:

```
[[[[ 0.54881352  0.59284461]
   [ 0.71518934  0.84426576]]]]
[[[[ -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38
     -3.40282347e+38]
   [ -3.40282347e+38   5.48813522e-01   5.92844605e-01   5.92844605e-01
     -3.40282347e+38]
   [ -3.40282347e+38   7.15189338e-01   8.44265759e-01   8.44265759e-01
     -3.40282347e+38]
   [ -3.40282347e+38   7.15189338e-01   8.44265759e-01   8.44265759e-01
     -3.40282347e+38]
   [ -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38  -3.40282347e+38
     -3.40282347e+38]]]]

```


As a possible fix, we were thinking about actually forbidding padding sizes greater or equal to the kernel sizes. After all, it's a meaningless operation, as it is creating values not dependent on the input. This could be applied to convolutions also.

And if someone really wants to do it anyway for some reason, the padding layer is already there for this special cases.

@sbodenstein @taliesinb

issue_title: SymbolBlock has no `_reg_params` which cause save/load params fails
issue_body:
## Description
block with symbol block as child doesn't save/load symbol block's parameters.

## Environment info (Required)

mxnet==1.2.0b20180425

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon as gl, nd
from mxnet.gluon import nn


class Net(gl.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Net, self).__init__()
        with self.name_scope():
            backbone = gl.model_zoo.vision.resnet50_v1()
            data = mx.sym.var('data')
            featnames = ['stage1_activation2', 'stage2_activation3', 'stage3_activation5']
            out_names = ['_'.join([backbone.name, featname, 'output']) for featname in featnames]
            internals = backbone(data).get_internals()
            outs = [internals[out_name] for out_name in out_names]
            self.backbone = gl.SymbolBlock(outs, data, params=backbone.collect_params())
            self.body = nn.Conv2D(3, 1)
            
    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        x = self.body(x)
        return self.backbone(x)
    
ctx = mx.cpu()
net = Net()
net.initialize(mx.init.Normal(), ctx=ctx)
net.hybridize()

net(nd.random.normal(shape=(1, 3, 224, 224)))
net.save_params('./test.params')
for k, v in nd.load('./test.params').items():
    print(k)

for k, v in net.collect_params().items():
    print(k)
```

add `_reg_params` to symbol block solves this problem.

Maybe a conditional parameter to check for inactivity and accessibility would help.

issue_body:
This works:

`$ assimp export laurana500_with-string.ply output.obj`
 
where the ply contains this line:

```ply
comment TextureFile laurana500.png
```

Whereas this does not:

comment TextureFile 5.png

The parser begins parsing the `5` as another type of line. I believe this behaviour was introduced in #1293

issue_title: IAM.Client.simulate_principal_policy gives incorrect results for admin
issue_body:
I'm building a CLI that allows users to upload data to S3 and I want to ensure that they have permission to upload to the location they specify (both for organization and to prevent typos). I figured out that this snippet does what I expect for most users:

```python
def validate_s3_path(s3_path: str):
    """
    Verifies that a given s3 path is valid for this user

    :param s3_path:
    :return: True if the user has access to the path, False otherwise
    :rtype: bool
    """

    prefix = "s3://"
    assert s3_path.startswith(prefix)

    s3_bucket, s3_prefix = s3_path[len(prefix):].split("/", 1)

    iam_client = boto3.client('iam')
    sts_client = boto3.client('sts')

    # Get the arn represented by the currently configured credentials
    arn = sts_client.get_caller_identity()['Arn']

    # Create an arn representing the objects in a bucket
    bucket_objects_arn = f'arn:aws:s3:::{s3_bucket}/{s3_prefix}*'

    # verify that this user can put objects in this location
    results = iam_client.simulate_principal_policy(
            PolicySourceArn=arn,
            ResourceArns=[bucket_objects_arn],
            ActionNames=['s3:PutObject']
    )

    return results['EvaluationResults'][0]['EvalDecision'] == 'allowed'
```

However, when I try to test it myself (using an admin account) I found a strange behavior: it seems to shortcut on the fact that I'm an admin and returns `"allowed"` even when the bucket isn't in our account.

This seems like a bug, although it may be a rare corner case. I can work around it by checking that the bucket in question is in the account, but I would expect this code to give me the correct permissions regardless of who owns the bucket. It might be useful to know that e.g. I have read access to a public public but not write access to it.

issue_title: Adding invalid blobs to Index leaves stray lock-file in python 3.5 due to non deterministic desctructors
issue_body:
(originally reported in #508)
`Index.add()` code relying on destructors leaves stray lock-file if invoked with invalid args in py3.5.  The following code reproduces it:

```
import git

r = git Repo()

def add_bad_blob():
    r.index.add([git.Blob(r, b'f' * 20, 'bad-permissions', 'foo')])

try:
    ## 1st fail on purpose adding into index.
    add_bad_blob()
except Exception as ex:
    """
    git\index\base.py in add()           @799
    git\index\base.py in write()         @213
    git\index\base.py in _serialize()    @180 
    git\index\fun.py in write_cache()    @133
    """
    assert "required argument is not an integer" in str(ex)

try:
    ## 2nd time fails due to stray lock-file.
    add_bad_blob()
except Exception as ex:
    assert "index.lock' could not be obtained" in str(ex)

import gc
gc.collect()


try:
    ## After GC, lock-file gone.
    add_bad_blob()
except Exception as ex:
    assert "required argument is not an integer" in str(ex)

```

The problem originates at `git/index/base.py#L211-214`, and can be amended by enclosing them in a `try-finally` block:

```
    stream = lfd.open(write=True, stream=True)
    ok= False
    try:
        self._serialize(stream, ignore_extension_data)
        ok = True
    finally:
        if not ok:
            lfd.rollback()
```

This issue is a show-stopper, and impossible to workaround even when applying the advice written in the manual about invokint ``__del__()` manually, because the `LockedFD` variable instance goes out of scope.

So either 
a) the project sources are modified, or 
b) try-catch and explicitly invoke GC then, which is pretty ugly.  

A more proper solution would be to retrifit `LockedFD` as a context-manager.

issue_title: Set topology settings as class attribute in DSL
issue_body:
The `Topology` DSL allows setting storm settings with the `config` parameter to components:

```
    pixel_counter = PixelCounterBolt.spec(name='pixel-count-bolt',
                                          inputs={pixel_deserializer:
                                                  Grouping.fields('url')},
                                          config={"topology.tick.tuple.freq.secs": 1})
```

However, it doesn't really make sense to set topology-level settings in particular components, and it introduces ambiguity since a topology-level setting could be set for multiple components. 

Perhaps `config` should be specifiable as a class attribute of a `Topology`?

[The commits since 0.1.2](https://github.com/gocardless/gocardless-pro-python/compare/v0.1.2...master) are backwards compatible and the resource changes since represent the current API. It would be great if the changes were published so that they can be used without installing directly from the repo with pip.

issue_title: Maximizing the number of detected sources
issue_body:
I would like to get rid of all non-Python dependencies in [our pipeline](https://github.com/vterron/lemon/), and the first one that I'm tackling is SExtractor. I've followed the section of the [photutils documentation on source detection](http://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/v0.2.2/photutils/detection.html), but I'm encountering a little problem: I cannot manage to get it running properly without knowing anything a priory about the image — such as, for example, the FWHM. Would it be possible to use photutils so that as many possible sources as possible are detected, via some sort of generous defaults? False positives would not be an issue, as in our case they would be automatically discarded at a later stage.

For comparison, the "blind settings" that we currently use with SExtractor identify 971 sources [on this image](http://www.iaa.es/~vterron/123m-CAHA_2012.01_raw_120108_ferM_0093.fits)

**Solution:**

issue_title: Tokens won't refresh after expiration date despite high refresh expiration delta
issue_body:
I've implemented `rest_framework_jwt` and have it working for authentication when accessing my API. However, I'm unable to refresh any tokens if it's after the token's `exp` value despite setting `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA` to 30 days.

For example, if I have the following settings...

    JWT_AUTH = {
        'JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA': datetime.timedelta(seconds=900),
        'JWT_ALLOW_REFRESH': True,
        'JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA': datetime.timedelta(days=30),
    }

...then shouldn't I be able to refresh the token within 30 days of the token's `orig_iat` date? Am I misreading the [settings documentation](http://getblimp.github.io/django-rest-framework-jwt/#additional-settings) for `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA`?

Out of curiosity I dug into PyJWT and found out that its `decode()` function only checks that the expiration date is later than `utcnow()+leeway`:

	if 'exp' in payload and verify_expiration:
	    utc_timestamp = timegm(datetime.utcnow().utctimetuple())

	    if payload['exp'] < (utc_timestamp - leeway):
	        raise ExpiredSignatureError('Signature has expired')

Going back into `RefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer` reveals that the token isn't getting past the initial payload check (which calls PyJWT's `decode()` function) to determine if the current time is within `orig_iat + RefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer`.

Is this just a huge misunderstanding on my part as to how JWTs work?

issue_title: Streamed decoding only provides unneccessarily huge chunks
issue_body:
When streaming encoding, it's working fine using small buffer-sizes (indeed only does so), thus presumably it should be able to decode in similarly small chunks, however the streaming decoder seems to only return 4.19mb chunks (i.e. i'm streaming data to BrotliDecompressBufferStreaming in 16kb chunks, but it only first returns anything whenever it has accumulated 4.19mb available). 

Given that this seems unneccessary (since the data was streamed on write in far smaller chunks), it would be very preferable to be able to have it return more frequent + smaller chunk sizes, as this kind of delay+chunkiness makes it unusable for some streaming scenarios, and less ideal for others.

issue_title: MemoryBuffer should manage its memory more automatically
issue_body:
Currently we store `MemoryBuffer*` objects everywhere, which is prone to memory leaks.

Instead, it is better to have `MemoryBuffer` a wrapper object, containing `MemoryBufferImpl*` pointer that it manages. The semantics should be similar to that of `std::shared_ptr`: allow `MemoryBuffer`s to be shallow-copyable, and destroy the underlying `Impl*` objects automatically when their reference count reaches 0.

issue_title: Spikes are counted outside the stimulus
issue_body:
Not sure if this should be reported as a bug or as a request for enhancement, depending on whether the behavior I'm reporting is intended or not. I used a snippet of code inspired from the GitHub main page:
```
        trace = {}
	trace['T'] = copy(t_vec)
	trace['V'] = copy(v_vec)
	trace['stim_start'] = [h.stim.delay]
	trace['stim_end'] = [h.stim.delay + h.stim.dur]
	traces.append(trace)
	traces_results = efel.getFeatureValues(traces, ['Spikecount'])
```
and it took me quite a while to realize that Spikecount (an many other features I guess) is actually not taken into account the stim_start and stim_end and will include in its count any spikes in the trace, regardless of the stimulus window. Is that intended? If not, it should definitely be corrected. If yes, I would suggest adding some boolean parameter allowing the users to specify if they want the features to be computed on the full trace or on the stimulus window only.

issue_title: Validator fails to determine mimetype when several are provided in spec.
issue_body:
### Description

I have an endpoint that produces either `application/json` or `text/csv`, depending on Accept header. 

This is reflected in swagger file as 
```
/endpoint:
    get:
      summary: get all calibrations from server
      operationId: endpoint
      produces:
        - application/json
        - text/csv
      responses:
        '200':
          description: 'OK'
          schema:
            type: array
            items:
              $ref: '#/definitions/CalibrationData'  ## this in an array of records of 3 floats
          examples:
            text/csv: |-
                  1, 0.12, -12
                  2, 1, 2
            aplication/json:
              Series: 1
              a: 0.12
              b: -12
      parameters: []
```

I have turned on response validation with ```app.add_api('api.yaml', validate_responses=True)```

A correct response to a request with `Accept: text/csv` header should be sent to browser. The ResponseValidator should pass text/csv as is.

Failed validating 'type' in schema: 
...
On instance:

### Steps to reproduce



### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

Python 3.5.2

- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`

Version: 1.4.2

from gcloud import bigtable

issue_title: Pub/Sub subscriber flow control stops ack requests
issue_body:
Pub/Sub client library stops sending Ack or modifyAckDeadline requests entirely when flow control settings are defined. See code below: when ran as it is against a subscription with a large backlog, it shows that the client thinks it's processing ~700 messages/second, while there is no outgoing traffic from the machine and the monitoring metrics show no ack | modAckDeadline traffic. If we comment out the flow control argument, the overall processing rate is the same, acks and modifyAckDeadlines are regularly and successfully received by the server. Expected behavior: flow control settings do not prevent the client library from acknowledging the message. 1. OS type and version Mac Os Sierra 2. Python version and virtual environment information `python --version` 2.7 3. google-cloud-python version `pip show google-cloud`, `pip show google-` or `pip freeze` pip show google-cloud-pubsub Name: google-cloud-pubsub Version: 0.28.4 4. Stacktrace if available 5. Steps to reproduce - Run without flow control setting against a subscription with a large (10K messages) backlog - Run without flow control setting against a subscription with a large (10K messages) backlog 6. Code example 
import time
import datetime
import sys
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1 as pubsub
import threading

subscription_name = "projects/%s/subscriptions/%s"%(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
sleep_time_ms = 0
try:
    sleep_time_ms = int(sys.argv[3])
except Exception:
    print "Could not parse custom sleep time."

print "Using sleep time %g ms"%sleep_time_ms
start_time = -1
messages_processed = 0.0
thread_lock = threading.Lock()


def callback(message):
    global start_time, thread_lock,messages_processed

    if start_time < 0 :
        with thread_lock: start_time = time.time()

    time.sleep(float(sleep_time_ms)/1000)
    message.ack()
    with thread_lock:
        messages_processed += 1


subscription = pubsub.SubscriberClient().subscribe(subscription_name, flow_control = pubsub.types.FlowControl(max_messages=100)).open(callback=callback)

while(True):

    time.sleep(5)
    print "Acking messages at rate %.5f / second"%( messages_processed / (time.time() - start_time))

issue_title: Discussing adding separate system tests for REST and gRPC.
issue_body:
Per what we talked about a little bit today.

It would probably be a good idea to run both the REST and gRPC code in the system tests.
I think most services that could have both REST and gRCP tests could use `skipTest` to manage when it is appropriate to run them.(https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/2648#discussion_r85767970)

### When to run them?
I think @dhermes brought up that we shouldn't run both of them all the time and that maybe we should use Travis-CI cron to run them once or twice a day. We could also just cron building master 1-4 times per 24 hours instead of on every merge.

issue_title: Add support for resources to GoWorkspaceTask
issue_body: Currently Go has no official way to deal with resource files.  Since pants constructs parallel workspaces under .pants.d, this means any relative path expectations used by non-pantsed Go code will fail.  To work around this / work with this, pants should respect `resources(...)` dependencies in Go targets when building a workspace symlink farm or else have a looser interpretation of acceptable files owned by a go target and just glob ~everything when building a workspace.

This will pick up manylinux support and should enable us to discontinue publishing a `pantsbuild.pants` source tarball (which is invalid - it contains a ~random embedded native engine resource, ie: an OSX or a linux native engine, but not both).

The proper support is via the `go get` <meta> protocol as described in #2378, but short-term support can be provided via a new set of default Fetchers & ArchiveFetcher configurations.

The ArchiveFetcher rule is just:
```python
{
  r'golang\.org/x/(?P<repo>[^/]+)':
    UrlInfo(url_format='https://github.com/golang/\\g<repo>/archive/{rev}.tar.gz',
            default_rev='master',
            strip_level=1)
}
```

issue_title: Conversion between Targets and their BUILD aliases.
issue_body:
There should be an easy way to do both:

1) Given a Target type, lookup what BUILD aliases map to it.
2) Given a BUILD file alias, lookup what Target type it corresponds to.

The motivation for this is allowing users to specify the names of BUILD file aliases (such as `java_library`) as options, which can be read and understood properly by tasks, subsystems, et cetera, rather than having to use internal names like `JavaLibrary`.

issue_title: Add support for new transit information
issue_body: http://googlegeodevelopers.blogspot.com.au/2015/01/transit-directions-improvements.html

When using this in setting where I need to make a lot of calls in quick succession. I'm seeing a performance issue with all the stopping and starting of clients connections.

If there was a way to start a session and re-use it for each connection, it would cut down the time spent fetching information dramatically.

It doesn't seem like it would be too hard augmenting the Client init to take in a passed session variable for re-use.

Is there any interest in this?

In the places class, the function places_nearby uses location+radius to search for places nearby, but in the [places API docs](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/javascript/places#place_search_requests) it also documents using a LatLng bounding box as type 'google.maps.LatLngBounds' instead of a location and radius.  But in the places_nearby function location is required and is converted to a latlng in _places:

`    if location:
        params["location"] = convert.latlng(location)`

This makes it seemingly impossible to send a bounding box, as it is not a single lat long, and there is not a param for bounds.  Is there a way to send a bounds object to the API instead of a location + radius with this library?

issue_body:
## Summary
Learner username is not displayed on the top right corner of the browser on a Portrait mode in a tablet.

**Important user story**
Its always good to have the learners name displayed all times at the top right corner, so that the learner is aware that he/she is using their own account. This is a big issue since we have't implemented the #2131 and because of this at-least 50% of the leaner end up progressing on the wrong student account. Some learners prefer using Portrait mode and some prefer Landscape mode, hence is enhancement is important.

 - Version: 0.4.5 and 0.5.0
 - Operating system: Ubuntu 14.04LTS
 - Browser: Chrome.

1. Login in a tablet on Landscape mode, notice the user name is clearly visible at the top right corner.

3. The learner has to got to profiles to know the username.

issue_title: ttl-security issues or I am confused
issue_body:
I am trying to use the ttl-security option and at least on FreeBSD it doesn't work.

I found a couple of issues:

First, in reactor/network/tcp.py, the call to io.setsockopt passes a hardcoded value for TTL, 20, rather than getting the ttl parameter. The correct line would be:
        io.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_IP, socket.IP_TTL, ttl)

Second, in reactor/outgoing.py I see that you call the TTL function from Outgoing.establish(). I understand that this function would be called, if used at all, after a connect() has been done. I am not sure if you can do a setsockopt() to change the TTL after the connection has been established, but at least it wasn´t working for me. 

I have added the call to TTL(self.io, peer, ttl) after the call to MD5() (before bind() and connect()) and now it works as it should. I've verified that my packets indeed have the right TTL value.

All that said, there are two different usages for the TTL in a BGP session. You can set it to a low value (which routers use to do as a default unless you specify a "multi-hop" session) or you can force packets to have a ttl of 255 and specify a *minimum TTL* of 255 so that the connection will drop packets with a TTL less than 255. In that case, however, the maximum allowed value for ttl-security should be 255, not 254.

And in order to be valid for both purposes, ttl-security should set *both* the connection TTL and the minimum TTL. Although I am checking and a setsockopt with IP_MINTTL doesn't work, it's not used by Python 2.7 despite being defined on FreeBSD. Maybe this is an OS dependent feature?

The TTL of 255 has an interesting property: it should avoid the resource consumption caused by a bombardment of TCP packets to a BGP listener using a MD5 key. Packets from a remote network would indeed have a TTL of less than 255 and they should be discarded before trying to verify the MD5 signature.

issue_title: match default glyph order when building TTFs from glyphs source
issue_body:
When generating a TTF (but for CFF-OTFs), Glyphs.app reorders the glyphs so that the first four are .notdef, null, CR and space, following an outdated recommendation in the TrueType spec (see Georg's comments in https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/issues/111).
It does this even if an explicit `glyphOrder` custom parameter is set. It doesn't do any reordering if `TrueType Keep GlyphOrder` or `DisableAllAutomaticBehaviour` are set.

If think that if fontmake wants to match Glyphs.app behaviour when building TTFs, it should do the same reordering.

Not that this can't be done in glyphsLib, because glyphsLib only produces UFOs, which in turn can be used to produce either TTFs or OTFs, and we only want this reordering to happen when building TTFs.
Neither this can be done in ufo2ft, as the latter already reads and applies the `lib["public.glyphOrder"]` whatever that is, following the UFO spec.

So it should be fontmake (one level above glyphsLib and ufo2ft) that should check when generating TTF from glyphs source file if the `TrueType Keep GlyphOrder` or `DisableAllAutomaticBehaviour` custom parameters are set; if not, it should reorder the special glyphs and pass a custom `glyphOrder` to `ufo2ft.compileTTF` function, which already accepts such an argument.

issue_title: BaseChainDB no longer supports a header-only DB
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

`BaseChainDB._set_as_canonical_chain_head()` now expects the DB to contain transactions as well, and that breaks `LightChain` since it only syncs headers.

When there's a chain reorg, that method will try to [remove tx lookups from the DB](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/evm/db/chain.py#L108-L110), but that crashes because we have no transactions in the DB

issue_title: Consider tuning solver package minimization passes
issue_body:
From @mingwandroid via https://github.com/conda-forge/pygridgen-feedstock/issues/10#issuecomment-365983589:


> We call these packages 'Core Dependency Tree' packages and they are never installed at runtime (only ever at build-time).
>
> @kalefranz, any comment on the solver issue? From what I know, I really dislike this minimize number of packages (or is it minimize change of number of packages?) heuristic as it naturally prefers less well split up package sets and static libraries over shared ones.

issue_title: Permission denied installing pip within conda environment
issue_body:
Trying to install `pip` into my environment, I run into issues because the package is held in a central place, and was last touched by someone else:

```
OSError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/projects/ps-yeolab/software/anaconda-2.1.0_2015-01-20/pkgs/pip-6.0.6-py27_0/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pip-6.0.6-py2.7.egg-info'
```

And indeed, this file is not owned by me (my username here is `obotvinnik`):

```
-rw-r--r-- 7 shsathe yeo-group 1.9K Jan  7 11:48 /projects/ps-yeolab/software/anaconda-2.1.0_2015-01-20/pkgs/pip-6.0.6-py27_0/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pip-6.0.6-py2.7.egg-info
```

Do you know why these permissions issues happen? It seems that if something is written to `anaconda/pkgs`, it should be writeable by everyone so that new versions can get installed.

https://gist.github.com/olgabot/2934dfc4fe2ad5de2c39

issue_title: Behavior changes to consider for conda 5.0
issue_body:
A running list of behavior changes to consider for the next major version bump of conda.

* Run solver for explicit installs by default #6147
* Removing many of the CLI flags for which help has been `SUPPRESS`ed.
* make the `-S` / `--satisfied-skip-solve` option added in #7291 default behavior

issue_title: Why is conda-env still added implicitly to Specs?
issue_body:
conda-env has been merged into conda but you still require it as a dependency (and conda forge even has an empty package for it).  Also it is implicitly added as spec when installing something (https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/plan.py#L735).

What's the reasoning behind this? Why not just remove all of this?

issue_title: Release notes at readthedocs not updated to 0.8.4
issue_body: http://pysam.readthedocs.org/en/stable/release.html seem to only have release notes up to release 0.8.3.

issue_title: Can't type a 'v' in Output window
issue_body:
I have searched for this issue in the forum. If it's been dealt with, it was hidden well. 

I loved OpenCobolIDE--for a while. Then I found that although the Output window would let me type stuff in in response to ACCEPT statements, I couldn't type a letter 'v', lower case or capital. Trying produced nothing, not even a blank space. Running the program in a terminal window gave no problem. So now I'm jumping back and forth from Gedit to a terminal window again, with no syntax highlighting. What's the deal with the letter 'v' anyway? Every other letter of the alphabet is fine.

issue_title: Cannot run 'no ip virtual-router mac-address' in eos 4.17.x
issue_body:
When running `make systests` the VARP-related tests will fail.  The command mentioned in the title is no longer allowed in 4.17.x, as you now have to specify which mac address to remove.

- [ ] Update Varp API
- [ ] Update Unit tests
- [ ] Update System tests

Took me a while to figure out why snapshotting stopped working.  I patched [these lines](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/976be3a8230eab595654f1c5017e4cca2c1f30d3/curator/api/snapshot.py#L63) to:

    except Exception as exc:
        logger.error('Failed to verify all nodes have repository access: %s', str(exc))

Not sure if prefer another way to log exceptions (eg logger.exception to show full traceback).  It should help us better understand the underlying cause:

    2016-01-28 18:16:54,863 ERROR     Failed to verify all nodes have repository access: TransportError(404, 'repository_missing_exception')

The above is not a curator problem - just wanted to show example.  Now to figure out how my repository definition was deleted...

issue_title: Allow use of date params in alias keys
issue_body:
I'd like to be able to have the following create_index action:

```
name: foo-%Y-%m-%d
extra_settings:
  aliases:
    foo-v1-%Y-%m%d: {}
    foo-v2-%Y-%m%d: {}
```

Curator supports strftime patterns in the name field, but not the aliases section.

Hi,

I'm trying to use es.update() on ES 2.1.1 and with a script and I'm having trouble.
```
es.index(index='boston', doc_type='stem_map', id=111, body={'word': 'showing', 'counter': 29})
es.get(index='boston', doc_type='stem_map', id=111)

{'_id': '111',
 '_index': 'boston',
 '_source': {'counter': 29, 'word': 'showing'},
 '_type': 'stem_map',
 '_version': 1,
 'found': True}
```
```
upscript = {
    'script': {
        'inline': 'ctx._source.counter += count', 
        'params': {
            'count': 100
        }
    }
}
```
Then I tried both of the following:
```
es.update(index='boston', doc_type='stem_map', id=111, body=upscript)
es.update(index='boston', doc_type='stem_map', id=111, script=upscript)
```
I'm getting the following error:

```
RequestError: TransportError(400, 'illegal_argument_exception', '[John Walker][127.0.0.1:9300][indices:data/write/update[s]]')
```
What am I doing wrong?

* Choices Enum version: 0.1.6
* Python version: 3.6.2
* Operating System: Linux

### Description

I'm porting existing Django code to use choicesenum. One existing use case that doesn't work is to use enum values as keys in a dict, because ChoicesEnum isn't hashable, like the stdlib enum implementation is.

### What I Did

``` python
>>> from enum import Enum, IntEnum
>>> from choicesenum import ChoicesEnum
>>> class Foo(Enum):
...     A = 1
...     B = 2
...     C = 3
...
>>> class Bar(IntEnum):
...     A = 1
...     B = 2
...     C = 3
...
>>> class Baz(ChoicesEnum):
...     A = 1
...     B = 2
...     C = 3
...
>>> Foo(1)
<Foo.A: 1>
>>> hash(Foo(1))
-8133611313320584089
>>> Bar(1)
<Bar.A: 1>
>>> hash(Bar(1))
1
>>> Baz(1)
Baz(1).A
>>> hash(Baz(1))                                                                                         
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: unhashable type: 'Baz'
```

issue_title: Allow user to specify RBAC on or off when installing aci connector
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
The `az aks install-connector` command doesn't allow the user to toggle if the cluster has RBAC or not. This means it uses the default value of the virtual-kubelet helm chart: `rbac is disabled`.

This poses a problem for new AKS clusters that have RBAC. When the `az aks install-connector` command is used, they end up with virtual-kubelet in a broken state because the needed RBAC related resources are not created. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**

The `install-connector` command should have an additional flag to allow the user to enable or disable rbac for the vk install. 

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

If we could determine if the cluster had RBAC, we could default this selection. 

**Additional context**

### Description
Many simple commands e.g. `az webapp list` or `az appservice plan list` fail with a Python error
```
/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cryptography/hazmat/bindings/_openssl.abi3.so: cannot enable executable stack as shared object requires: Invalid argument
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/azure/cli/main.py", line 36, in main
```
I've just updated to the latest v2.0.12 CLI. The `create` options also fail

apt-get under WSL

Bash for Windows

issue_title: Documented, Tested Examples of Idempotent Resource Creation
issue_body:
In light of #5006, I think the community would benefit greatly from having canonical examples of how to write scripts to do things like "ensure resources exist".

These types of scripts are used all over the place, and having a documented method for idempotent resource creation is table stakes, in my opinion.

Further, I've been personally broken by changes in the CLI, due to the fact that there was either no guidance around this, no testing around it, or both.

Additionally, someone on Twitter indicated that I might be better off using `az resource show` (rather than `az [resource] show` as it would then be more likely to be consistent about how it handles 404s. Which is a great point, and would be a great thing to see written down in some guidance and example bash scripts.

(Apologies if these such examples exist, I've not been able to find them yet!)

The IoT module should support the `--ids` parameter to allow users to specify the necessary names or a fully qualified resource ID string for commands like `show`, `delete`, etc.

As part of this, I will add a discussion of this feature to the command authoring guide.

issue_body:

When I try to send an argument containing ';' character in params dict of session.post, the ';' character seems to be considered as a separator instead of the message content.

params dict should work even if it contains a ';' character

Hi,

First of all, thanks for making autograd! It's been extremely useful.  

I noticed though that np.take produces the wrong result if the index array contains duplicate values.  For example, 
`quick_grad_check(lambda x: np.sum(x[[0,0]]), np.array([1.]))`
fails with:
`AssertionError: Check failed! nd=2.0, ad=1.0`

You can fix it by changing a line in `primitive_sum_arrays` (in `numpy_extra.py`) from
`new_array[array.idx] += array.val`
to:
`numpy.add.at(new_array, array.idx, array.val)`.  I'm not sure if this error occurs elsewhere, but it seems to at least fix the problem in all of the cases that I've tried.

However, keep getting error.

Moreover, This error is not experienced if I use the pip version of the repository.

I noticed sonnet has provided a module called LayerNorm, which is based on the article https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450.
However, when I look at the corresponding implementation of TensowFlow, it provides a class called LayerNormBasicLSTMCell (TensorFlow does provides a standalone version of layer normalization tf.contrib.layers.layer_norm).
According to my understanding, and when I check the code in https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/r1.3/tensorflow/contrib/rnn/python/ops/rnn_cell.py:

```

    i, j, f, o = array_ops.split(value=concat, num_or_size_splits=4, axis=1)
    if self._layer_norm:
      i = self._norm(i, "input")
      j = self._norm(j, "transform")
      f = self._norm(f, "forget")
      o = self._norm(o, "output")


```

What I mean here is the layer normalization is done INSIDE the cell, before activation. However, when sonnet provides the solo LayerNorm module in sonnet, in which way it is intended to be used? Like the following way:

```
import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt

class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)

        hidden_size_array = [8, 8, 8]
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._layer1 = snt.LSTM(hidden_size_array[0], name = 'layer1_lstm')
            self._norm1 = snt.BatchApply(snt.LayerNorm(), name = 'layer1_norm')

            self._layer2 = snt.LSTM(hidden_size_array[1], name = 'layer2_lstm')
            self._norm2 = snt.BatchApply(snt.LayerNorm(), name = 'layer2_norm')

            self._layer3 = snt.LSTM(hidden_size_array[2], name = 'layer3_lstm')
            self._norm3 = snt.BatchApply(snt.LayerNorm(), name = 'layer3_norm')


    def _build(self, inputs):

        batch_size = inputs.get_shape()[0]

        initial_state = self._layer1.initial_state(batch_size)
        output_sequence, final_state = \
            tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._layer1, inputs, initial_state = initial_state, time_major = False)

        output_sequence = self._norm1(output_sequence)

        initial_state = self._layer2.initial_state(batch_size)
        output_sequence, final_state = \
            tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._layer2, output_sequence, initial_state = initial_state, time_major = False)

        output_sequence = self._norm2(output_sequence)

        initial_state = self._layer3.initial_state(batch_size)
        output_sequence, final_state = \
            tf.nn.dynamic_rnn(self._layer3, output_sequence, initial_state = initial_state, time_major = False)

        output_sequence = self._norm3(output_sequence)

        return output_sequence

def test():

    t = tf.constant(np.ones((4, 6, 8)), tf.float32)
    model = Model()
    outputs = model(t)

    with tf.Session() as sess:
        sess.run([tf.global_variables_initializer()])

        v = sess.run(outputs)
        print(v)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test()

```

Nevertheless in this way it can work, however, seems layer normalization is done outside the activation of LSTM cell. 

So I just wonder the dear engineers at DeepMind could give a doc a little detailed with regarding to the usage of LayerNorm module or not? (Or even probably my understanding of layer normalization is wrong). But I would like to hear the opinion of engineers there, so by clarification we can have sonnet easier to use.

Thanks a lot.

issue_title: Subordinate charm downgrades charms.reactive breaking primary
issue_body:
A PostgreSQL charm is installed happily, using the default of no venv, and has charms.reactive 0.5 installed into /usr/local.

A canonical-livepatch subordinate is installed, also not using a venv, and the charms.reactive in /usr/local is downgraded to 0.4.7. This breaks the primary charm:

```
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:39 DEBUG install Processing ./wheelhouse/charms.reactive-0.4.7.tar.gz
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:39 DEBUG install Requirement already up-to-date: pyaml in /usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages (from charms.reactive==0.4.7)
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:39 DEBUG install Building wheels for collected packages: pip, six, netaddr, charmhelpers, MarkupSafe, charms.reactive
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:42 DEBUG install   Running setup.py bdist_wheel for charms.reactive: started
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:42 DEBUG install   Running setup.py bdist_wheel for charms.reactive: finished with status 'done'
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:42 DEBUG install Successfully built pip six netaddr charmhelpers MarkupSafe charms.reactive
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:42 DEBUG install Installing collected packages: pip, six, netaddr, charmhelpers, MarkupSafe, charms.reactive
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:43 DEBUG install   Found existing installation: charms.reactive 0.5.0
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:43 DEBUG install     Uninstalling charms.reactive-0.5.0:
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:43 DEBUG install       Successfully uninstalled charms.reactive-0.5.0
unit-canonical-livepatch-5.log:2017-10-23 20:43:43 DEBUG install Successfully installed MarkupSafe-1.0 charmhelpers-0.18.0 charms.reactive-0.4.7 netaddr-0.7.19 pip-8.1.2 six-1.10.0
```

This issue will affect all deployments with primaries and subordinates mixing charms.reactive 0.5 and pre-0.5, and at least one charm not using the virtual env option. If any of the charms depend on the 0.5 API, then the deployment will break. It may not break immediately, depending on which order the install hooks run, but will certainly break when upgrade-charm's are run later.

issue_title: EncodedField and EncodedMethod have no method '_reload'
issue_body:
### Describe what you wanted to do
I wanted to call the method `set_name()` on an EncodedField, and later on an EncodedMethod, to set the in-memory name for a method that had been set to a single-letter, presumably by ProGuard.

- is this error specific to a single file?
Yes, andguard/core/bytecodes/dvm.py

- if possible give a minimal working example where the error happens
Presuming you have an APK loaded as `a`, the DEX loaded as the DalvikVMFormat `d`, and that the APK has a class `ExampleClass` with field `a`

```python
d.C.LExampleClass.F.a.set_name('new_name')
```

### Describe what you expected
The field referred to as `d.C.LExampleClass.F.a` is now referenced as `d.C.LExampleClass.F.new_name` and shown as `new_name` whenever the field is used, such as when calling `str()` or looking up the Xrefs

### Describe what actually happened
```python
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-36-5f433d2fe809> in <module>()
----> 1 d.C.La.F.a.set_name('new_name')

C:\Android\re_tools\AndroguardMaster\androguard\core\bytecodes\dvm.pyc in set_name(self, value)
   2786     def set_name(self, value):
   2787         self.CM.set_hook_field_name(self, value)
-> 2788         self._reload()
   2789 
   2790     def get_obj(self):

AttributeError: 'EncodedField' object has no attribute '_reload'
```

### System Information

- Androguard Version: master
- Python Version: 2.7 Anaconda
- Operating System: Windows 7 64-bit Home

### Further Log Files and Output

- please report logs, tracebacks and other useful information
- if possible report the files you tried to analyze!

commit 9d519eaae6542 changed the call from `self.reload()` to `self._reload()`  EncodedField and EncodedMethod still have `reload` methods.  This same commit added `_reload()` methods to other classes in the file, such as `FieldIdItem` and `MethodIdItem`.  However, `EncodedField` and `EncodedMethod` did not have their reload functions changed.

issue_title: Referencing .NETStandard,Version1.3 NuGet library depending on System.ComponentModel.TypeConverter from .NET 4.7
issue_body:
When I reference the NuGet package SharpSnmpLib version 9.2.2 from a .NET 4.7 app it fails during execution with:

```
2017.08.21 10:05:35.217   ERROR System.IO.FileLoadException: Could not load file or assembly 'System.ComponentModel.TypeConverter, Version=4.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' or one of its dependencies. The located assembly's manifest definition does not match the assembly reference. (Exception from HRESULT: 0x80131040)
```

It seems like this may have something to do with to the .NETStandard,Version1.3 dependency on the System.ComponentModel.TypeConverter NuGet package.

This seems like it could be related to #730 but I'm not sure.

One workaround I found based on a suggestion from @weshaggard in #730 is to manually add NuGet references to System.ComponentModel.TypeConverter and System.Runtime.Serialization.Primitives. This seems problematic though because these types are already included in the full .NET 4.7 framework so it doesn't seem correct to add a separate reference to a different version of them. Also it seems like if these references are necessary they should be automatically added by NuGet.

What's the expected behavior here?

# Steps to reproduce:

1. Create a .NET Framework Console App referencing version 4.7.
2. Add a NuGet reference to Lextm.SharpSnmpLib version 9.2.2.
3. Add this code in Main():

        static void Main() {
            Console.Out.WriteLine(typeof(ObjectIdentifier).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(Attribute)));
            Console.ReadLine();
        }
4. Run the Console Application.

We originally ran into this issue using the Machine.Specifications test runner to run tests in a project that references SharpSnmp lib. It looks like the test runner uses GetCustomAttributes to find test attributes.

issue_title: metakernel-bash should work with Cygwin under Windows
issue_body:
I tried using metakernel-bash on Windows8.1/64 with my Cygwin bash.
I was intrigued by the error message when trying with metakernel-bash ... giving me hope that this is doable.

I was getting an error message similar to
    /usr/bin/bash  'ls\r' Command not found.

Seeing '\r' gave me hope.

Digging into the code I changed one line in pexpect.py, in 'class spawnu' in the 'PY3' branch of the
if statement, I changed line1905 from
    linesep = os.linesep
to
    linesep = '\n'

and this was sufficient to have only a single cell work (the last command of a cell).

This is maybe dependent upon the line-ending choice chosen at Cygwin installation time.

It would be useful to be able to override os.linesep, at least for cygwin bash.

issue_title: Powerline for Zsh not connecting to daemon
issue_body:
My powerline prompt in Zsh does not seem to be connecting to the daemon.

```
% cat /etc/lsb-release
DISTRIB_ID=Ubuntu
DISTRIB_RELEASE=16.04
DISTRIB_CODENAME=xenial
DISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS"
```

```
% zsh --version
zsh 5.1.1 (x86_64-ubuntu-linux-gnu)
```

```
% pip show powerline-status | grep '^Version'
Version: 2.5
```

```
% file ~/.local/bin/powerline
/home/xandor/.local/bin/powerline: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, for GNU/Linux 2.6.32, BuildID[sha1]=ae4f0504ed8a97f976490efd936f45a11b4fd61a, not stripped
```

`powerline-daemon` is started by Tmux (which is run before Zsh); `.tmux.conf`:
```
PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
run-shell "powerline-daemon -q"
run-shell 'tmux source-file "$(python -m site --user-site)/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf"'
```

Relevant part of `.zshrc`:
```
PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
source "$(python -m site --user-site)/powerline/bindings/zsh/powerline.zsh"
```

(`PATH` is exported later in `.zshrc`. Incidentally I'm actually adding `$HOME/.local/bin` to `PATH` only once because the setting in `.tmux.conf` is necessary for Powerline to work for Tmux, but it doesn't export to the shell env, which is exactly the behaviour I want).

`~/.config/powerline/config.json`:
```
{
	"common": {
		"term_truecolor": false
	},
	"ext": {
		"shell": {
			"colorscheme": "solarized",
			"theme": "default",
			"local_themes": {
				"continuation": "continuation",
				"select": "select"
			}
		},
		"tmux": {
			"colorscheme": "solarized",
			"theme": "default"
		}
	}
}
```

I'm using `libzpython`. Contrary to the README of zpython, debian-based OSs do have the correct headers in the `zsh-dev` package to be able to build. I built it against Python 2.7 (using `cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local -DPYTHON_INCLUDE_DIR=/usr/include/python2.7 -DPYTHON_LIBRARY=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpython2.7.so && make && sudo make install`).

I've been struggling with the performance of Powerline for Zsh on my Ubuntu box for a little while, wondering what the problem was. I edited `~/.local/bin/powerline-daemon` to print some statements in the `do_run` function. When I then ran `powerline-daemon --foreground` (after killing it first), there is no output from the shell client. Tmux client was definitely connected, lots of activity indicated by my added print statements. When I removed the powerline bindings from `.tmux.conf` and again looked for activity from the shell, there was simply no output from `powerline-daemon`, indicating no connections from any clients.

I'm not using Powerline for any other software at the moment.

Please advise :-).

issue_title: sync: upload error is not retried nor does it exit with error
issue_body:
b2 client 0.5.4. (updated to 0.5.6 when editing comment and reporting point 3)

Today I got a lot of connection reset errors while performing a sync command:

```
b2_upload(/other/localbackup/duplicity-full.20160526T124808Z.vol55.difftar.gpg, duplicity-full.20160526T124808Z.vol55.difftar.gpg, 1464271210848): UnknownError() unknown error: SysCallError(104, 'ECONNRESET')
```

On the webapp, I see a "started large file" with 0 bytes and that name.

I don't know why, I suppose some transient network issues from my network to backblaze servers, but the real issue is:
1) The sync command does not retry to upload the file, ever.
2) The sync command does not exit with an error status in such situation! Since my purpose is to employ the b2 client as an unattended uploader for backups, that's quite an issue!
3) launching again the sync command, it will do nothing; it won't try to resync the wrongly uploaded files - I must force the --compareVersions size option!

IMHO:
1) the sync command should retry a certain number of times the upload if the error is transient (or  maybe, if it's hard to understand wheter it's transient, it could retry a certain number of times and reset the counter if at any time succeeds);
2) if ANY unrecovered error happens during the sync, the exit status from the tool should be != 0; I think this is really critical in production environments.
3) If some error happens, a removal of the destination large file should be attempted, to prevent failed uploads to be corrected by further syncs (or any other measure that can prevent such behaviour would be ok).

Also after running several times the `predict` function with same parameters / inputs, the order of items in top-10 of recommendations changes a lot. (For example, a item can appear in the 6th position, and another time in the 3rd position). Does it mean the model has not trained enough ?

Thanks in advance for your answers,

Julien

I am just wondering how ```pvwatts_dc``` method is intended to be used. Looking at the test, it sounds like we have to create a custom ```module_parameters``` with ```pdc0``` and ```gamma_pdc```. Those two parameters are not present in the standards module objects retrieved from CECMod or SandiaMod. 
```pdc0``` looks like being more a parameter tied to a group of modules rather than a module alone. Could it be calculated from the standard ```module_parameters``` entered to the instance in combination with ```modules_per_string``` and ```strings_per_inverter```?. It would perhaps be more "system like", and we would have a use of the instance calculating ```pvwatts_dc```.

As well, there is no ```eta_inv_nom``` / ```eta_inv_ref``` in the ```inverter_parameters``` object retrieved from CECMod. Calculating ```pvwatts_ac``` is not going to work straight. Maybe it could be more explicit that it has to be manually inserted (raise a custom error if not present ?).

issue_body:
In the docs the examples for the `CustomRecurrentLayer` and the docs don't match. Particularly - ``
```
input_to_hidden : :class:`lasagne.layers.Layer`
        :class:`lasagne.layers.Layer` instance which connects input to the
        hidden state (:math:`f_i`).  This layer may be connected to a chain of
        layers, which must end in a :class:`lasagne.layers.InputLayer` with the
        same input shape as `incoming`. 
```
However, in the examples to `incoming` has shape `(n_batch, n_steps, n_in)` while the bottom `InputLayer` of the `input_to_hidden` has shape `(None, n_in)`. The same applies an the example of convolutional recurrence where the `incoming` has shape `(n_batch, n_steps, n_channels, width, height)`, while the bottom of the `input_to_hidden` has shape `(None, n_channels, width, height)`. From this it seems that the `InputLayer` of the `input_to_hidden` must have shape `incoming.shape[1:]`

Sry Itfish :/

Today i'm coming with something i noticed lately. It's about the knowledge base / functions for statically linked ARM-32 binaries. It's not a bug I think, well maybe it's both a bug and a quality problem.

Situation: 
I have a function - starting at some Address, let's say 0xC. This function is correctly identified in the knowledge base, however it's got exactly 1 block instead of 6.
And then .. i have a second function in the knowledge base, lets say Address 0xA. And it has the needed 6 blocks. However, the function starts with an `andeq foo`, meaning a nop. The correct function entry 0xC has a correct [usually used] function prologue `push stuff` but missing blocks. And this does not happen only with andeqs, but also with.. other stuff like `bleq, andhi, ...`.

`In [10]: len(list(b.kb.functions.get(0x80C0C).blocks))`
`Out[10]: 1`

`In [3]: b.factory.block(0x80C0C).capstone.pp()`
`0x80c0c:	push	{r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, sb, sl, fp, lr}`
`0x80c10:	ldrb	r7, [r0, #5]`
`0x80c14:	sub	sp, sp, #0x1c`
`0x80c18:	ldrb	r6, [r0, #4]`
`0x80c1c:	ldrb	r3, [r0, #1]`
`...`

`In [11]: len(list(b.kb.functions.get(0x80C08).blocks))`
`Out[11]: 6`
`In [5]: b.factory.block(0x80C08).capstone.pp()`
`0x80c08:	andeq	sl, r7, r0, ror r8`
`0x80c0c:	push	{r4, r5, r6, r7, r8, sb, sl, fp, lr}`
`0x80c10:	ldrb	r7, [r0, #5]`
`0x80c14:	sub	sp, sp, #0x1c`

For testing purposes i wrote a badly written and possible idiotic testing skript to preprocessing functions. I want the functions with the correct number of blocks. So basically what i do is to check all function entries, record them in a dictionary. If a function entry has weird andeqs, eors, ... at the beginning i remove than and recalculate the entry address. If the entry address is already recorded in the function dict, i'll check which of the two functions has the bigger number of blocks. And it yields way better results. 

    `func_dict = {}         
    for f in b.kb.functions.items():
        entry = f[1].get_node(f[0])
        if entry is not None and check_function_entry(b, f) == True:
            func_entry_block = b.factory.block(f[0])            
            
            # NEW
            #print "real", real_entry_addr
            i = 0                
            while i < len(func_entry_block.capstone.insns) and func_entry_block.capstone.insns[i].insn.mnemonic == 'andeq' : # maybe add eor
                i += 1
    		            
            new_func_entry = func_entry_block.capstone.addr + i*4
            
            #print "new", new_func_entry
            
            if func_dict.has_key(new_func_entry) == True:
                if b.kb.functions.get(new_func_entry) is not None and len(list(b.kb.functions.get(f[0]).blocks)) >  len(list(b.kb.functions.get(new_func_entry).blocks)):
                    func_dict[new_func_entry] = f
            else:
                func_dict[new_func_entry] = f´

`In [6]: funcs = analysis.semantic_transform.function_sanatizer.pre_prepare_funct
   ...: ions(b, 0)`
`In [7]: len(funcs)`
`Out[7]: 7964`

`In [8]: len(b.kb.functions.items())`
`Out[8]: 9224`

The reduction with only removing beginning andeqs from entry blocks and acceping only the funciton with a larger number of blocks leads to a function reduction of 1200. So this basically is no small quality issue. 

I'm not sure if thats intended, or on the todo list. I think for in the long run a predefined function prologue option for the knowledge base would be good, or move predefined prologue checks in process irrational functions. 

Greetings
